item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I live in Java, and I can safely say that in Javanese language, the descriptor comes after the noun. So it should be Kuthamegachile instead of Megachilekutha.

Or you can use -kerta instead to achieve the same result. Megachilekerta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the houseflies and the continuous implication that this is just one instance of a larger interconnected phenomenon. But there are a few problems with how the flies are described that detract from the article for me.

  • The text alternately refers to "the house fly" (singular) and "these house flies" (plural). If the Foundation isn't sure whether the same fly is coming back every month, they should acknowledge that uncertainty, not just use the singular and plural interchangeably. This issue can probably be largely avoided by addressing the next point.
  • The flies are clearly anomalous, but they aren't given a designation of their own. Referring to it/them as simply "these flies" conflicts in a very basic way with one of the few consistent bits of tone and writing style throughout the site: if it's got anomalous properties, it gets a designation, and it's referred to exclusively by that designation. You don't even have to shoehorn them into the first description paragraph- just give them a designation when you first mention them, and use it in the rest of the article. ("A modified house fly (Musca domestica), hereafter SCP-2044-3, arrives at the end of every month…")
  • The flies are the most obviously weird thing about this whole process, and yet show little to no sign of having been investigated. The lice are sentient and industrialized and Polish, sure, OK- but they're contained, and available for study anytime. The flies have to come from somewhere. I can understand if the Foundation's investigations hit a wall somewhere (the fly vanishes into/appears from thin air; the fly accelerates to speeds that make it practically impossible to track; the fly consistently finds and escapes through surveillance blind spots, even when none should exist; the fly locates and mingles with non-anomalous houseflies to evade pursuit; …) or even if they're still working on it and haven't found anything so concrete yet. Right now it just says "attempts have been unsuccessful"; even "attempts have thus far been unsuccessful" would be better than that, since it implies they're still trying.

Overall I love the concept and enjoy the article, but these problems are pretty significant. No vote for now.

On a separate note, the explosive detonation is completely believable to me. My theory is that the Foundation didn't find the device when they brought 2044-1 in because it wasn't there at the time; 2044-2 installed it after [they/the big wigs/someone else] realized 2044-1 was being investigated, so they could stop him if he decided to spill the beans. When 2044-1 was ambulatory, the Foundation wasn't observing 2044-2 all the time- it would be easy for a few specimens to occasionally take some parts delivered by the fly, wander down to the base of the neck, and do some construction work without any outside observers noticing. 2044-1 might have noticed, but he was already pretending not to know all sorts of things about 2044-2, and certainly might have kept quiet about this as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The world will be that of producer and consumer and those who refuse will be industrialized. We are to fulfill our destiny and become one with the free market...

No market is too small for the Factory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if bees had corporate competition?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man. I love this concept. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this is different. Usually I hate 'zomg funny industry cryptic company' things but the image of a small factory being in this guys hair is crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reminds me of that one episode of Foster's Home for Imaginary Friends where Eduardo gets infested with imaginary, sentient fleas, and the rest of the gang tries to give him a flea bath.

Edit: Damn it somebody else noticed that too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel fairly neutral on this one. (no up/downvote)

The housefly idea seems to come out of nowhere and it seems silly that an insect's buildings would be recognizable enough to be compared to human buildings.

But on the other hand, I like how the fly connects this small "head world" to something even bigger outside, and the fact that the buildings are so humanoid emphasizes that this oddity was apparently man-made.

But when I realize this, I feel less surprise ("Oh my god!) and more like "oh. ok."
Maybe it was just how I read it, but it doesn't seem tight enough? I'm afraid I can't suggest much more than just tweaking the tone?
Currently it feels cutesy to me, and despite the apparent threat, a group making insects build itsy-bitsy towns doesn't convince me. Am I missing something? The only disconcerting thing about this (to me) is the fact a parasite is acting so human. And the overwhelming urge to brush my hair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Before long the Foundation is gonna have another occult organization to deal with: the Big Wigs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the interviews are translated from Polish, is there a reason why you left "bękart" untranslated instead of translating it into "bastard"? The plural for the word is bękarty, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The only constructs not composed of materials not native to the human scalp is a large factory complex on the outskirts of the community and three track loaders."

Double negative, I got what it meant but it kinda snagged my eyes which disrupted the reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really disconcerting.

I'm going to go rigorously shampoo now. +1

item.title: Re: My First SCP
item.content:encoded: Just edit the page to put a name next to the link to SCP-2044.

item.title: Re: My First SCP
item.content:encoded: Oh. how do I do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like Scantron said, there's a lot of handwaving going on here and I especially don't buy that a bomb/explosive wouldn't have been found. On the other hand, it's a neat idea and you executed it well enough that I won't downvote. No voting for now, might change my mind later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but I just don't buy this. I don't buy miniaturized versions of normal things working right or being delivered by randomly appearing flies (the Foundation could at the very least put him in a clean room and watch the flies appear out of nowhere), I don't buy the Foundation thinking the dude wouldn't realize there was a fucking factory on his head, I don't buy them not finding the explosive device during a physical or an MRI, I don't buy the overly cute names, I don't buy them giving him a bed that could be propped up against the door, I don't buy the Foundation not recognizing that something was up before the dude snapped.

Too much is handwaved, not enough makes sense. And it doesn't really stand out from our other sapient insects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not doing it for me currently. I'm on the edge of liking it, but also on the edge of not liking it as far as voting goes. Think it's just a bit too silly for me. What with the head lice foreman, the list of buildings visible, the houseflies, etc. If it was a bit more, mysterious? I'm not talking about the "big wigs" but if the lice acted more foreign, more alien, if we saw structures on the head but they weren't recognizable for example. Interview also doesn't do much to help with this for me.

Needs fine tuning imho.

item.title: Re: My First SCP
item.content:encoded: You should probably name this on the Series III page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WE LOVE YOU EDWARDO Pretty neat. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, fixed. Thanks for the heads up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot.

It made my head itchy though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a huge fan of this, it's not terribly written, but at the same time, it's not written well enough either. That and I'm just getting burnt out on the 'weird for the sake of weird' articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, I'm a bit of a sucker for SCPs that are "things that are on/in human bodies that shouldn't be" and I felt this one was good, upvoted accordingly

Also you might want to fix the SCP-2044-B spoiler/collapsible thing, when clicked there's a XXXX-B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is cool, but the writing is just a little too off for me. There are a few tonal issues, and some lolfoundation-like moments scattered throughout this that I'm not really a fan of. Also, I doubt that a small explosive device would be precise enough to knock someone into a comatose state without just straight-up killing them.

Downvote, because the writing isn't good enough to back up the strong idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Besides the errors mentioned by Aelanna, you have some other apostrophe placement issues (such as "sit's"). Overall I didn't find the idea captivating enough to upvote and some of the dialogue seemed a tiny bit off to me, but I couldn't really find it in myself to downvote it either. + 0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have "its"/"it's" mixups and the note in the Procedures is borderline LOLFoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel it could be a little bit longer, but over all I think it's very soild!
Fairly original, and really dig the visual of a tiny tiny town on a dude's head.
The fly-helicopter repurposed really sold it for me, though. I was just kind of nodding along expecting some wierd dark twist- AND THEN BAM- Helifly carrier shows up. Took it back to a good place for me. +1 all the way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was strong in the sandbox, and it's strong here. Well done for making a solid first entry! I look forward to seeing a lot more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, it's interesting.

item.title: My First SCP
item.content:encoded: Okay, here is my first SCP. After weeks of trying to come up with a good idea, finding a way of putting it into words, and submitting it for peer review, I think it's ready. Please, any criticism is welcome.
